driver.body: The first three drivers of Payments built trust; this one turns that trust into cash. Kepler takes the flows the earlier drivers defended, underwrote, and made cash-free, and monetises them through four routes — interchange, take-rates, credit yield, and, increasingly, data-adjacent products [S-061]. The strategic subtlety sits in one distinction: interchange is regulated and varies by region, so the monetization ceiling is set by policy, not by ambition [S-061]. The durable margin therefore does not live on the swipe — it lives in the parts of the stack regulators do not cap [S-061]. That single split — the capped swipe on one side, the uncapped trust-products on the other — organises every mechanism below, and it is why Payments' monetization is inseparable from the whole flywheel rather than a rate business run on its own.

mechanisms.body:
Interchange is not one number; it is a patchwork of regional caps, and the monetization model has to be built region-by-region or it breaks [S-062]. The asymmetry is stark across Kepler's footprint [S-412]:

- In the EU/EEA the Interchange Fee Regulation sets hard statutory caps — **0.20% on debit and 0.30% on credit** [S-412]. There is almost no rate to play with, so the model must monetize on volume and adjacencies, not on the swipe itself.
- In Singapore and much of South-East Asia there is no hard statutory cap, but scheme-set and competitive pressure lands the effective blended rate at ~0.5–0.9% [S-412].
- In India, merchant-side MDR on UPI is effectively zero by policy [S-412]; monetization here is never interchange — it is credit, float, and value-added services.
- In Brazil, the PIX instant-payment rail carries no interchange while the Central Bank actively compresses card economics [S-412]; the conclusion is the same as India's.

Crucially, US-style uncapped credit interchange of ~1.8–2.2% does not exist anywhere in Kepler's markets, so the model must never assume it [S-412]. The implication is the load-bearing one: across roughly **half of Kepler's TPV, interchange is legally near-zero** [S-412]. Monetization therefore cannot rest on the swipe. It has to rest on the trust-adjacent products — credit yield, insurance, merchant lending, data-informed underwriting — that regulators do not cap. This is exactly why Payments cannot be a monoline: in its two largest-by-population markets the swipe is free by law, so the only monetization path is the one that requires the whole flywheel — lending and services priced off cross-domain trust [S-062]. In the source's own framing, trace S-412 is the reason Driver 2 exists [S-062].

Kepler monetizes across four layers, each carrying a different regulatory exposure, and the point of laying them out as a stack is to show where the margin is actually durable [S-063]. The swipe-linked layers — interchange and take-rate — sit exposed to the caps of Mechanism 4.1. The trust-linked layers — credit yield and float/services — sit above them, priced off behaviour that regulators do not touch.

The decisive figure is that the two high-durability layers, credit yield and float/services, already contribute **61% of Payments gross profit despite representing under 40% of TPV** [S-413]. That inversion is the whole point: monetization has already migrated off the regulated swipe and onto the trust-moated products. The stack is not a plan for the future — it is a state Payments has already reached.

The customer statement is where all four layers are experienced at once, and it is a trust artefact as much as a financial one [S-063]. The credit score is visible and moving; the APR fell because behaviour earned it — the credit engine talking back to the customer; and the fraud line advertises that protection ran without wrongly blocking her. The statement is where **T, the trust term of the moat, is made felt** [S-063]. And trust, once shown, sells the next product: surfacing the earned-APR line lifted repeat credit uptake **17%** [S-414]. The interface is not decoration laid over the monetization — it is part of the monetization engine.

Beneath the statement sits the raw ledger — the same ledger from Driver 1, now read not as a record of flows but as a record of margins [S-064]. A single day's mixed flows carry very different economics, and the join — one user, many domains — is what makes the portfolio work [S-064].

Read across the user column and the whole monetization thesis appears in miniature. rivera_a and courier_301 each touch three domains in a single day [S-415], and the high-margin entries — credit yield running **700–1240 bps** [S-415] — are only underwritable because the low-margin entries, the rides and the jobs, supplied the behavioural collateral that made the underwriting safe. Neither half works alone: a lender without the ride-and-job history cannot price the credit, and the ride-and-job history earns almost nothing until a credit line monetizes it.

That is the mechanism at its sharpest: **the cheap flows are the moat, the expensive flows are the monetization, and the ledger is the join that makes one pay for the other** [S-415]. The proof point closes the loop. Contribution per interaction inside Payments moved from −0.19 over the trailing period [S-416] — the clearest single evidence that the flywheel's target path up from the −0.31 blended figure is achievable, and that the path runs straight through Domain 4 [S-416]. The ledger is where the monetization stops being a story a conglomerate tells and becomes a number a flywheel can show.

component_data.title: Unified ledger — one day, mixed flows across domains

component_data.meta: Read down the user column: rivera_a and courier_301 each touch three domains in one day [S-415]. The thin-margin rides and jobs supply the behavioural collateral that makes the 700–1240 bps credit yield underwritable — the ledger is the join that makes the cheap flows pay for the expensive ones [S-415].
